content.text: React's rendering model is built on a simple promise: if the state reference has not changed, the UI does not need to update. That promise only holds when state is treated as immutable — never modified in place, always replaced with a new object or array. Understanding immutability and the closely related concept of pure functions is therefore not optional background knowledge for React developers; it is a prerequisite for state updates to work correctly.

value cannot be changed after it is created. Primitive types in JavaScript — numbers, strings, booleans — are already immutable: you cannot alter the character at index 2 of a string; you can only create a new string. Objects and arrays, however, are
by default — their properties and elements can be changed in place.
When we talk about treating state as immutable in React, we mean deliberately choosing
to mutate objects and arrays even though JavaScript allows it. Instead, every update produces a new reference — React then compares the old and new references to decide whether to re-render.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of mutation patterns (avoid in React) versus immutable update patterns (React style) for objects, arrays, and functions
attrs.title: Mutation vs. Immutable Update Patterns

For deeply nested state, this becomes verbose. In React projects you can use the
library (used internally by Redux Toolkit) which lets you write mutating syntax that actually produces immutable updates under the hood.
Array methods in JavaScript fall into two groups: those that mutate the original array, and those that return a new array. In React state updates, always use the non-mutating versions.
Always returns the same output for the same input.
No randomness, no time-dependence, no external reads.
It does not modify any external state, write to a database, call an API, or mutate its arguments.

React treats function components as pure functions of their props and state. Given the same props, a component must always return the same JSX. React's Strict Mode deliberately calls components twice in development to catch impure renders.
Event handlers (onClick, onSubmit) are intentionally impure — they run in response to user interaction, not during render. Data fetching and subscriptions belong in
, not in the render function itself.
hook (used for complex state) requires a reducer — a pure function that takes the current state and an action and returns new state without mutating the original.
Immutable updates are not as expensive as they might look. When you spread an object, the parts of the state that
still share the same reference — they are not copied. Only the path from the root to the changed node becomes new.
and know that the settings subtree has not changed, skipping any components that only depend on settings.
library lets you write mutating code that is transparently converted to immutable updates. It is especially useful for deeply nested state where manual spreading becomes unwieldy.
makes an object non-writable at runtime — attempts to mutate it silently do nothing in non-strict mode or throw in strict mode. It is shallow, like spread.
Mutating in useEffect or event handlers

Even though React does not call event handlers or effects during render, mutating state objects in these places still breaks things — because the state variable held by other closures points to the now-mutated object.
Immutability and referential equality in React hooks

) depend on referential equality checks. They only skip work if their dependencies have not changed. Immutable updates make these checks reliable — if you mutate instead of replacing, the memoised value never knows to update.
would see the same array reference and serve a stale cached result. Immutable updates ensure memoisation caches invalidate correctly.
Writing pure utility functions for state
Extracting state transformation logic into pure utility functions keeps components lean and makes the transformations easy to unit-test independently.
Pure functions are the easiest code to test — no setup, no mocks, no async. Every test is just "given these inputs, expect this output".
React detects state changes by comparing references. Mutating an object in place produces the same reference — React will skip the re-render.
The spread operator is shallow — spread at every nesting level you intend to change.
return the same output for the same input and have no side effects — components and reducers must be pure.
Unchanged subtrees share the same reference (structural sharing), so immutable updates are efficient.
provides an ergonomic way to write immutable updates using familiar mutating syntax.
